title: Incorporate sPlots in combine template fit

posts.name: Riccardo Manzoni
posts.text:
Dear Experts,
I have the following problem: the observable that I want to fit to measure a given signal, in red, is q2 (plot attached). There is a large contamination from some combinatorial background, in grey, which however can be singled out using the invariant mass of a known resonance (the other plot attached) and sPlots.

is there a way to incorporate sPlots and corresponding sWeights in a multidimensional fit, q2 and invariant mass, within combine?

does combine allow to fit weighed data?

Let me clarify that, for the time being, and I have not made any concrete attempts yet, just interested to know if the idea might work, in principle, and if there are examples that I can follow.
Thanks,
Riccardo

q2696×672 8.08 KB

mass696×672 9.13 KB

posts.name: Riccardo Manzoni
posts.text:
“the end justify the means”
Jokes apart, I’m am also more than curious to know if there are better ideas to achieve the same goal.
Cheers,
Riccardo
posts.cooked_html:
“the end justify the means”

Cheers,
Riccardo

posts.name: Pietro Vischia
posts.text:
yes, it’s always a compromise between need and ideality.
Jokes aside, the most tricky part is checking for independence rather than only uncorrelatedness, and the issue with uncertainties.
We had a workshop on this a couple years ago https://indico.cern.ch/event/909987/ , and I seem to remember also another in-depth workshop where a modification to splot was proposed but I need to find the link.
Cheers,
Pietro

Jokes aside, the most tricky part is checking for independence rather than only uncorrelatedness, and the issue with uncertainties.

posts.name: Julie Malcles
posts.text:
Hi Riccardo,
You can do a 2D fit directly in fact (as done in HH-> bb gamma gamma analysis for example), you do not need sPlot.
sPlot would rather be doing a 1D fit with invariant mass only to estimate the q2 distribution of signal or background. You are not supposed to use the variable you want to infer with sweights
as a discriminating variable in the fit in sPlots.
Cheers,
Julie

Hi Riccardo,

You can do a 2D fit directly in fact (as done in HH-> bb gamma gamma analysis for example), you do not need sPlot.

sPlot would rather be doing a 1D fit with invariant mass only to estimate the q2 distribution of signal or background. You are not supposed to use the variable you want to infer with sweights
as a discriminating variable in the fit in sPlots.

Cheers,

Julie
